nativeRange.habitat: Blackwater and clearwater tributaries and flooded forest streams (igapós) in the upper Amazon basin; tannin-stained, warm, very soft, acidic water with leaf litter, submerged roots, and dense riparian shading

decorPreferences: dark substrate (fine sand or dark gravel)
driftwood and dried leaf litter for tannins and natural blackwater effect
dense planting or floating plants to diffuse light
open midwater swimming lanes for schooling behavior

diet.notes: Accepts quality micro-pellets, nano flakes, and small frozen or live foods: baby brine shrimp, daphnia, micro worms, and bloodworms (chopped). Small mouth dictates fine particle sizes. Color-enhancing foods high in carotenoids help maintain the red head marking. Feed once or twice daily in small portions.

goodWith: other small peaceful tetras (cardinal tetra, neon tetra, ember tetra)
avoidWith: fast boisterous fish that cause chronic stress
fish with mouths large enough to swallow them

imageCandidates.notes: FishBase species image for Petitella georgiae; check current availability on page for Peger_u0 or equivalent identifier.

summary: A schooling blackwater tetra closely resembling H. rhodostomus and H. bleheri; distinguished by a shallower red cap that stops at the operculum and by additional black caudal-fin markings. One of three species sold interchangeably as 'Rummy-Nose Tetra' in the trade.
careNotes:
Petitella georgiae is one of three species sold interchangeably under the name 'Rummy-Nose Tetra' in the aquarium trade — a persistent source of confusion for hobbyists. The three species can be distinguished as follows: Hemigrammus bleheri ('Bleher's Rummy-Nose' / 'Firehead Tetra', fw-fish-183) has the most extensive red coloration, which covers the entire head and extends onto the body; Hemigrammus rhodostomus ('Common Rummy-Nose', fw-fish-125) is intermediate, with red confined to the head and snout; and Petitella georgiae ('False Rummy-Nose', this entry) has the least red — the red cap ends at or before the operculum edge and does not bleed onto the gill cover. P. georgiae is also distinguished by additional black markings in the caudal fin beyond the central black stripe shared by all three species. Husbandry requirements across all three are essentially identical.

Like its congeners, P. georgiae is a tight schooler that displays more synchronized behavior in larger groups — 10 or more individuals are commonly recommended for optimal display. Red head coloration is a widely reported informal water-quality indicator: pallor or fading is associated with elevated nitrates, ammonia spikes, or pH instability. Regular partial water changes (25–30% weekly) are generally considered essential.

This species thrives in soft, acidic blackwater conditions mimicking its upper-Amazon origin. Tannin-staining via driftwood, Indian almond leaves, or botanical tea is beneficial. Hard or alkaline water shortens lifespan and suppresses coloration. Dimmed lighting and a dark substrate reduce stress. The species has historically been scarce in trade relative to H. rhodostomus and H. bleheri, so identification at point of sale is unreliable; buyers should inspect the extent of red on the head and the caudal pattern to confirm species if identification matters.
breedingNotes: Breeding in captivity is considered difficult and rarely accomplished. Like H. rhodostomus, P. georgiae is an egg scatterer; a dedicated breeding tank with very soft, acidic water (pH 6.0–6.5, gH 1–4), minimal lighting, fine-leaved plants or a spawning mop, and a mesh bottom to protect eggs from the adults is the standard approach. Spawning typically occurs in the evening; adults must be removed promptly as they will consume eggs. Eggs hatch in approximately 24–36 hours; larvae are tiny and require infusoria or commercial micro fry foods before graduating to freshly hatched brine shrimp nauplii. Sexual dimorphism is subtle — gravid females appear slightly deeper-bodied.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Petitella georgiae
primary.notes: Primary source. Confirmed scientific name, family Characidae, order Characiformes, adult size ~5 cm, temperature 24–28°C, pH 5.5–7.0, hardness 1–10°H, native range (upper Amazon, Peru and Brazil), blackwater biotope, omnivore diet, peaceful schooling temperament, minimum group 8–10. Confirmed distinction from H. rhodostomus (reduced red cap, additional caudal black) and difficult breeding. Also confirmed Petitella georgiae retains Characidae placement (not moved to Lebiasinidae).

additional.name: FishBase — Petitella georgiae
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Characidae, Characiformes — Lebiasinidae placement not current), adult size (to 5.5 cm TL), temperature (22–28°C), native range (Amazon basin, Peru and Brazil), depth habitat, and diet (omnivore). Confirmed family as Characidae; Lebiasinidae placement mentioned in some older sources is not reflected in FishBase current taxonomy.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Rummy Nose Tetra Care Guide
additional.notes: General rummy-nose care guide applicable across all three trade species including P. georgiae. Confirmed temperature 72–82°F (22–28°C), pH 6.0–7.0, soft water preference, schooling minimum 6+ (used 8 as conservative), peaceful temperament, omnivore diet, and tight schooling behavior as water-quality indicator. Notes interchangeable trade labeling of the three rummy-nose species.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Rummy-Nose Tetras (species group overview)
additional.notes: Species-complex identification guide. Confirmed the three-species trade confusion and provided the key morphological distinctions: P. georgiae has the smallest red area (not reaching operculum) and the most distinctive additional caudal black pattern; H. bleheri has the most extensive red; H. rhodostomus is intermediate. Also confirmed that 'False Rummy-Nose' and 'True Rummy-Nose' labels have been inconsistently applied across older literature, with P. georgiae sometimes labeled 'True' in older European aquarium texts.

additional.name: FishBase — Petitella georgiae taxonomy cross-check
additional.notes: Verified current accepted classification: Order Characiformes, Family Characidae. Some older sources (pre-2000) placed Petitella in or near Lebiasinidae; this is not the current accepted placement. FishBase and SeriouslyFish both list Characidae.

fish.breedingNotes: Egg scatterer; requires very soft acidic water (pH 6.0–6.5), fine-leaved spawning medium, and mesh bottom. Adults eat eggs and must be removed promptly. Fry are tiny and require infusoria initially.
